title: Thermal Inversion Simulator
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Split-screen particle simulation comparing normal atmospheric conditions with thermal inversion, showing how pollutants accumulate when trapped by a warm air layer

learningObjectives: Explain how thermal inversions trap pollutants near the ground by comparing normal and inverted temperature profiles
Analyze the relationship between emission rates, atmospheric conditions, and air quality
Connect thermal inversion events to real-world smog and public health impacts
prerequisites: Basic understanding of air pollution sources
Familiarity with temperature and altitude relationships

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Animated particle simulation with side-by-side comparison
keyQuestions: How does the temperature profile of the atmosphere affect pollutant dispersal?
Why does a thermal inversion act as a 'lid' trapping pollution near the ground?
What geographic features make some cities more vulnerable to inversions?
How do weather forecasts help cities prepare for poor air quality events?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think thermal inversions are caused by pollution, when actually inversions are weather phenomena that trap existing pollution
Students may believe that reducing emissions during an inversion has no effect, when in fact lower emissions mean less accumulation under the lid
Students sometimes think warm air always rises, not understanding that it only rises when it is warmer than the surrounding air
assessmentOpportunities: Have students create comparison charts of particle behavior under normal vs. inversion conditions
Ask students to research a historical smog event and explain the role of thermal inversions
Students design a public health advisory policy for a city prone to inversions
